agent_output:
# Food Delivery Platform вҖ” Architecture Design

## Executive Summary

A food delivery platform connecting restaurants, customers, and delivery drivers is a classic distributed systems challenge with real-time coordination at its core. The architecture uses an event-driven microservices approach organized around four bounded contexts (Ordering, Restaurant, Delivery, Identity), with a real-time event bus as the nervous system. This design optimizes for independent team scalability, real-time responsiveness, and the ability to evolve each context independently вҖ” the three things that matter most for a marketplace platform.

---

## Architecture

### Components

| Component | Responsibility | Boundaries |
|-----------|---------------|------------|
| **API Gateway** | Request routing, rate limiting, auth token validation, protocol translation (REST/WebSocket) | Does NOT contain business logic. Thin layer only. |
| **Identity Service** | User registration, authentication, authorization, profile management for all three actor types | Owns user identity. Does NOT own domain-specific data (e.g., restaurant menus, driver vehicles). |
| **Order Service** | Order lifecycle management: creation, payment orchestration, status tracking, order history | Owns order state machine. Does NOT own menu data or delivery assignment. |
| **Restaurant Service** | Menu management, restaurant profiles, hours/availability, order acceptance/preparation tracking | Owns menu and restaurant operational data. Does NOT decide delivery logistics. |
| **Delivery Service** | Driver matching, route optimization, real-time driver location tracking, delivery assignment | Owns delivery assignment and driver location. Does NOT own order payment state. |
| **Payment Service** | Payment processing, refunds, restaurant payouts, driver payouts, commission calculation | Owns financial transactions. Does NOT make business decisions about order flow. |
| **Notification Service** | Push notifications, SMS, email вҖ” reacts to domain events | Owns no business state. Pure reactor to events. |
| **Real-Time Gateway** | WebSocket connections for live order tracking, driver location streaming | Stateless connection manager. Subscribes to events on behalf of clients. |

### Interfaces

| From | To | Contract | Coupling |
|------|------|----------|----------|
| API Gateway | All Services | REST/gRPC, JWT bearer token | Sync, loose (routing only) |
| Order Service | Restaurant Service | `OrderPlaced` event вҶ’ Restaurant accepts/rejects | Async, loose (event) |
| Restaurant Service | Delivery Service | `OrderReadyForPickup` event вҶ’ triggers driver dispatch | Async, loose (event) |
| Delivery Service | Order Service | `DeliveryStatusChanged` event вҶ’ order status updated | Async, loose (event) |
| Order Service | Payment Service | `OrderConfirmed` вҶ’ charge; `OrderCancelled` вҶ’ refund | Async, loose (event) |
| Payment Service | Order Service | `PaymentSucceeded` / `PaymentFailed` events | Async, loose (event) |
| All Services | Notification Service | Domain events вҶ’ notifications | Async, loose (fan-out) |
| Delivery Service | Real-Time Gateway | Driver location stream (pub/sub) | Async, loose |

### Data Flow

```
Customer places order
    в”Ӯ
    в–ј
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ   OrderPlaced    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  Order    в”Ӯ в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–ә в”Ӯ  Restaurant     в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  Service  в”Ӯ                  в”Ӯ  Service        в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ                   в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
    в”Ӯ                                   в”Ӯ
    в”Ӯ OrderConfirmed              OrderAccepted /
    в–ј                             OrderReadyForPickup
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ                           в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  Payment  в”Ӯ                          в–ј
в”Ӯ  Service  в”Ӯ                  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ                   в”Ӯ  Delivery       в”Ӯ
                               в”Ӯ  Service        в”Ӯ
                               в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
                                        в”Ӯ
                                  DriverAssigned
                                  DriverEnRoute
                                  DeliveryCompleted
                                        в”Ӯ
                                        в–ј
                               в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
                               в”Ӯ  Notification    в”Ӯ
                               в”Ӯ  Service         в”Ӯ
                               в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
                                   (all events)
```

### Patterns Used

| Pattern | Rationale |
|---------|-----------|
| **Event-Driven Architecture** | The core flow (order вҶ’ restaurant вҶ’ delivery) is inherently asynchronous. Events decouple services so each can evolve independently. |
| **Saga (Choreography)** | Order fulfillment spans multiple services. A choreographed saga avoids a central orchestrator bottleneck while maintaining eventual consistency. |
| **CQRS (Order Service)** | Orders are read 100x more than written (customer tracking, driver checking, restaurant dashboard). Separate read models optimized for each actor. |
| **API Gateway** | Single entry point for clients. Handles cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting) without polluting services. |
| **Pub/Sub for Location** | Driver location updates are high-frequency, low-value-per-event. Pub/sub with TTL avoids storing stale data. |
| **Strangler Fig (future)** | If migrating from a monolith, services can be extracted one at a time behind the gateway. |

---

## Domain Model

### Bounded Contexts

| Context | Classification | Aggregates |
|---------|---------------|------------|
| **Ordering** | Core | `Order`, `Cart`, `OrderHistory` |
| **Restaurant** | Core | `Restaurant`, `Menu`, `MenuItem`, `PrepQueue` |
| **Delivery** | Core | `Delivery`, `DriverProfile`, `DriverLocation`, `Route` |
| **Identity** | Supporting | `User`, `CustomerProfile`, `RestaurantOwnerProfile`, `DriverProfile` |
| **Payment** | Generic | `Transaction`, `Payout`, `Refund` |
| **Notification** | Generic | `NotificationTemplate`, `NotificationLog` |

### Key Aggregates and Invariants

**Order Aggregate**
- Invariants: Order total must match sum of items Г— prices at time of placement. Order cannot transition backward in state machine. Cancellation only allowed before restaurant accepts.
- State machine: `Draft вҶ’ Placed вҶ’ Confirmed вҶ’ Preparing вҶ’ ReadyForPickup вҶ’ InDelivery вҶ’ Delivered | Cancelled`

**Restaurant Aggregate**
- Invariants: Menu items must have positive prices. Restaurant can only accept orders during operating hours. Prep time estimates must be positive.

**Delivery Aggregate**
- Invariants: A driver can only have N active deliveries (configurable). Assignment requires driver to be in "available" status. Delivery cannot complete without pickup confirmation.

### Context Map

```
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  Customer-Supplier  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  Ordering   в”Ӯ в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–ә в”Ӯ Restaurant  в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  (upstream) в”Ӯ                     в”Ӯ(downstream) в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ                     в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
       в”Ӯ                                   в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ Customer-Supplier                 в”Ӯ Customer-Supplier
       в–ј                                   в–ј
в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ                     в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
в”Ӯ  Payment    в”Ӯ                     в”Ӯ  Delivery   в”Ӯ
в”Ӯ  (ACL)      в”Ӯ                     в”Ӯ(downstream) в”Ӯ
в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ                     в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

Identity в—„в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ Shared Kernel (user ID) в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–ә All Contexts
```

- **Ordering вҶ’ Restaurant**: Customer-Supplier. Ordering dictates what data it sends; Restaurant conforms to the order event schema.
- **Ordering вҶ’ Payment**: ACL. Payment is a generic subdomain; the Order Service translates domain concepts into payment commands via an anti-corruption layer.
- **Restaurant вҶ’ Delivery**: Customer-Supplier. Delivery conforms to restaurant's "ready" signal.
- **Identity вҶ’ All**: Shared Kernel. User ID is the only shared concept; each context maintains its own profile projection.

---

## Trade-offs

| Decision | Gains | Sacrifices | Alternatives Considered |
|----------|-------|------------|------------------------|
| **Choreographed saga over orchestration** | No single point of failure, services are truly independent, easier to add new reactions | Harder to visualize full flow, debugging requires distributed tracing, compensating transactions are implicit | Orchestrated saga (centralized control, easier to reason about, but creates coupling to orchestrator) |
| **Event-driven over synchronous** | Loose coupling, resilience to partial failures, natural audit log | Eventual consistency (customer may see stale state briefly), more complex infrastructure | Synchronous REST calls (simpler but creates tight coupling and cascading failures) |
| **Separate Delivery Service** | Independent scaling (delivery is the most compute-intensive вҖ” matching + routing), separate team ownership | More network hops, data duplication of order address | Embedding delivery logic in Order Service (simpler but conflates concerns) |
| **CQRS for Orders** | Each actor (customer, restaurant, driver) gets a read model optimized for their view; write model stays clean | Eventual consistency between write and read models, more infrastructure | Single model (simpler but queries become complex and slow as actors have different needs) |
| **Generic Payment subdomain** | Can swap payment providers without touching core logic; keeps financial compliance isolated | Integration effort with ACL, potential for payment state to drift from order state | Embedded payment logic (faster to build initially, nightmare to change providers later) |

---

## Flexibility Points

| Area | Evolution Path |
|------|---------------|
| **Payment provider** | ACL isolates payment provider specifics. Swap Stripe вҶ’ Adyen by reimplementing the adapter, zero core logic changes. |
| **Matching algorithm** | Driver matching is encapsulated in the Delivery Service. Can evolve from simple nearest-driver to ML-based optimization without touching other services. |
| **Notification channels** | Event-driven; add new channels (WhatsApp, in-app) by subscribing to existing events. Zero changes to emitting services. |
| **Menu structure** | Restaurant context owns menus entirely. Can add modifiers, combos, dietary filters without affecting ordering (order captures a snapshot at placement time). |
| **New actor types** | e.g., "Corporate accounts" вҖ” add a new profile type in Identity, new read model in Ordering. Core flow unchanged. |

### Rigidity Points (Intentional)

| Area | Reason |
|------|--------|
| **Order state machine** | This is the core business invariant. Transitions must be strictly enforced вҖ” bugs here mean incorrect charges, lost deliveries, or broken trust. |
| **Event schema contracts** | Published events are contracts. Breaking changes require versioning (e.g., `OrderPlaced.v2`). This rigidity prevents cascading failures. |
| **User ID as shared kernel** | Changing user identity structure would ripple through every context. Keep it minimal and stable (UUID only). |
| **Financial transaction immutability** | Payment records are append-only. Never mutate a transaction вҖ” create compensating transactions instead. Regulatory and audit requirement. |

---

## Risks

| Risk | Severity | Mitigation |
|------|----------|------------|
| **Event ordering / delivery** | Critical | Use a partitioned event bus (e.g., Kafka with order ID as partition key). Idempotent consumers with deduplication. Dead-letter queues for failed processing. |
| **Saga failure / partial completion** | High | Define compensating actions for every saga step. Timeout-based detection of stuck sagas. Monitoring dashboard for saga completion rates. |
| **Driver location at scale** | High | Use Redis with geo commands for real-time location, TTL for auto-expiry. Don't persist every ping вҖ” aggregate for analytics separately. |
| **Distributed tracing complexity** | Medium | Mandate correlation IDs in all events and API calls from day one. Use OpenTelemetry. This is not optional вҖ” it's infrastructure. |
| **Menu/price consistency** | Medium | Snapshot menu item prices into the Order at placement time. The order is the source of truth for what was charged, not the current menu. |
| **Cascading failures** | Medium | Circuit breakers on all synchronous calls. Bulkheads to isolate failures. Graceful degradation (e.g., show cached menu if Restaurant Service is down). |

---

## Implementation Guidance

### Recommended Approach

Start with a **modular monolith** organized by bounded context, with an in-process event bus. This gives you all the architectural benefits (clean boundaries, event-driven flow) without the operational cost of microservices on day one. Extract to separate services when a specific context needs independent scaling or team ownership.

### Suggested Technology Stack

- **API Gateway**: Kong or AWS API Gateway
- **Event Bus**: Start with in-process (e.g., MediatR/.NET, Spring Events/Java, or a simple EventEmitter/Node.js). Graduate to Kafka/SNS+SQS when extracting services.
- **Real-time**: WebSockets via Socket.IO or AWS AppSync
- **Driver Location**: Redis with `GEOADD`/`GEOSEARCH`
- **Database per context** (logical separation in monolith, physical when extracted):
  - Ordering: PostgreSQL (transactional integrity)
  - Restaurant: PostgreSQL
  - Delivery: PostgreSQL + Redis (hot location data)
  - Payment: PostgreSQL (audit trail)

### Module/Service Structure (Modular Monolith Start)

```
src/
в”ңв”Җв”Җ ordering/
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ domain/          # Order, Cart aggregates
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ application/     # Use cases, saga participants
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ infrastructure/  # Persistence, event publishing
в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ api/             # REST controllers
в”ңв”Җв”Җ restaurant/
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ domain/
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ application/
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ infrastructure/
в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ api/
в”ңв”Җв”Җ delivery/
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ domain/
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ application/
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ infrastructure/
в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ api/
в”ңв”Җв”Җ payment/
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ domain/
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ application/
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ infrastructure/  # Payment provider ACL lives here
в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ api/
в”ңв”Җв”Җ identity/
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ domain/
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ application/
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ infrastructure/
в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ api/
в”ңв”Җв”Җ notification/
в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ ...
в””в”Җв”Җ shared/
    в”ңв”Җв”Җ events/          # Event schemas (Published Language)
    в””в”Җв”Җ kernel/          # User ID type, Money value object
```

### Pitfalls to Avoid

- **Don't share databases across contexts.** Even in a monolith, use separate schemas. This is the #1 thing that prevents future extraction.
- **Don't put business logic in the API Gateway.** It should route and validate tokens, nothing more.
- **Don't model delivery assignment synchronously.** It's inherently async (finding a driver takes time). Trying to make it sync creates terrible UX and fragile timeouts.
- **Don't skip event versioning from the start.** Version your events (v1, v2) even if you think they won't change. The cost is trivial; the regret of not doing it is enormous.
- **Don't store current prices in orders.** Snapshot at placement time. Price changes should never retroactively affect existing orders.
- **Don't build a real-time system without observability.** Distributed tracing and correlation IDs are not "nice to have" вҖ” they're required infrastructure.

---

## Confidence Assessment

| | |
|---|---|
| **Confidence Level** | **85%** |
| **High Confidence** | Bounded context boundaries (Ordering/Restaurant/Delivery/Payment are natural seams), event-driven approach (proven at Uber/DoorDash/Deliveroo scale), modular monolith as starting point (avoids premature distributed systems tax) |
| **Low Confidence** | Optimal driver matching algorithm (needs real data and iteration), exact saga compensation logic (depends on business rules around cancellation windows, partial refunds), scale thresholds for service extraction (depends on actual traffic patterns) |
| **Assumptions** | Platform operates in a single geographic region initially. Order volume is < 100K/day at launch (modular monolith can handle this). Three distinct user types (no hybrid roles like a restaurant owner who also delivers). Payment provider is external (Stripe/Adyen), not built in-house. |
